data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A sudden interruption of the Mediterranean thermohaline circulation caused by a massive Black Sea flood around 1220 BC led to a rapid regional aridification, starving the agrarian bases of Mycenaean, Hittite, Ugaritic and Levantine societies.", "mechanism": "Prolonged drought triggered by loss of warm Atlantic inflow, reducing river discharge and precipitating a cascade of settlement abandonment across the Eastern Mediterranean.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city‑states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "High‑resolution speleothem and lake‑core records from the region show no significant drop in precipitation during 1200‑1150 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "The hypothesis draws on recent marine‑sediment reconstructions of Black Sea outburst floods and models of their impact on the Mediterranean overturning circulation, offering a climate‑driven collapse that does not rely on the hyperarid narrative common in the literature."}
